ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Apr-02-2020 Continued To Jun-16-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Against Plaintiff, Nickodem Rudzinski, And His Counsel, Robert Abel, Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 128.7
Off Calendar Mar-03-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Aimee G. Hamoy, Esq. of BURNHAM BROWN, T:510.835.6815, for defendant/moving party;
Robert B. Abel, Jr., Esq. of THE LAW OFFICES OF ROBERT B. ABEL, JR., T:415.461.5400, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC., RASIER, LLC, AND RASIER-CA, LLC'S Motion For Sanctions Against Plaintiff, Nickodem Rudzinski, And His Counsel, Robert Abel, Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure Section 128.7 as follows:

Defendants Uber Technologies, Raiser, and Raiser-CA's motion for sanctions against Plaintiff pursuant to Code of Civ. Proc. 128.7 is denied. Under section 128.7, an attorney who "present[s] to the court, whether by signing, filing, submitting, or later advocating, a pleading, petition, written notice of motion, or other similar paper" certifies that it has legal and factual merit. Defendants are correct that section 128.7 does not apply just to an initial complaint, but by its clear language also applies to "later advoca[cy]" and "motion" practice. However, section 128.7 only sanctions the lack of legal or factual merit in materials that are actually "present[ed] to the court." (See Trans-Action Commercial Investors, Ltd. v. Firmaterr, Inc. (1997) 60 Cal.App.4th 352, 369 ["section 128.7 has no application to tactics not embodied in papers presented to the court"].) Defendants do not contend that Plaintiff's complaint, as against them, was frivolous when he filed it. Rather Defendants' argument is that the lawsuit became frivolous once Defendants explained that the car at issue was not involved with Uber. Plaintiff has not "present[ed]" anything to the court" since that time. Defendants' own cited authority actually undermines their position: "Under Rule 11 even though an action may not be frivolous when it is filed, it may become so if . . . the attorney continues to file papers supporting the client's claims." (Peake v. Underwood (2014) 227 Cal.App.4th 428, 441.)

The purpose of 128.7 also supports denial of Defendants' motion. Defendants argue that "Uber has been forced to retain counsel to defend this meritless claim, [and] it has incurred further costs and fees with being forced to bring this Motion" after Plaintiff was given an opportunity to dismiss. (Defs.' MPA 8: 23-24.) However, that is not the purpose of section 128.7. Section 128.7's "primary purpose is to deter filing abuses, not to compensate those affected by them." (Musaelian v. Adams (2009) 45 Cal.4th 512, 519.)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, email:tonyvaughn@yahoo.com
